description: Japanese Go terminology tips displayed to users during puzzle sessions. Each tip includes romanized terms with poetic English definitions, filtered by difficulty level. https://senseis.xmp.net/?GoProverbs

tips.text: Liberty (Dame): The breath of a stone. Without it, the stone perishes.
Atari: The final warning. One liberty remains before capture.
Ko: The rule of repetition. You must play elsewhere before recapturing.
Eye (Me): A single safe point. Two eyes grant absolute life.
False Eye (Uso-me): A deceptive shape. It lacks the strength of a true eye.
Life (Ikiru): Two eyes secure the group. It cannot be removed.
Death (Shisu): A group without eyes is lost. It returns to the bowl.
Territory (Ji): Empty space surrounded by stones of a single color.
Influence (Atsumi): Power projected across the board from a strong position.
Sente: The power of initiative. Force the opponent to respond to your lead.
Gote: The necessity of response. Maintain balance by following the lead.
Tenuki: The global vision. Play away from a local fight to gain greater value.
Tesuji: The sharpest tactic. Use technical brilliance to solve a local crisis.
Joseki: The balanced path. Established corner play where both sides prosper.
Fuseki: The opening map. Stake your claim while the board is still vast.
Hoshi: The Star Point (4-4). The fourth line prioritizes influence and speed.
Komoku: The 3-4 Point. A balanced start seeking roots and territory.
Sansan: The 3-3 Point. A direct claim to the corner's deep territory.
Keima: The Knight's Move. A swift, flexible leap for escape or pursuit.
Kosumi: The Diagonal Move. A solid, unbreakable connection.
Hane: The Bend. Strike around the edge of an opponent's stone.
Nobi: The Stretch. Gain a liberty and strengthen your position.
Takefu: The Bamboo Joint. An unbreakable connection across a one-point gap.
Tsumego: The study of life and death. The foundation of reading power.
Seki: Mutual life. A stalemate where neither side can strike safely.
Miai: Points of parity. If the opponent takes one, you take the other.
Aji: Lingering potential. A hidden weakness waiting to be exploited.
Sabaki: The art of lightness. Settle a group within the opponent's sphere.
Yose: The endgame phase. Finalize the borders with precision.
Dame: Neutral points. territory that belongs to neither side.
Snapback (Utogaeshi): A tactical sacrifice that captures right back.
Net (Geta): Capture by surrounding without direct contact.
Ladder (Shicho): A diagonal chase that ends at the board's edge.
Semeai: A capturing race. A contest of breaths between two weak groups.
Ikken-tobi: The one-point jump. The most balanced way to extend.
Ponnuki: The flower shape. Four stones capturing one, radiating power.
Tsuke: The attachment. A direct touch that creates strength and complexity.
Kiri: The cut. Severing the opponent's stones to create two weak groups.
Komi: Compensation points given to White to balance the first-move advantage.
Moyo: A large, potential framework of territory.
Nakade: The killing placement. A stone inside the eye space that prevents life.
Damezumari: Liberty shortage. When your own stones run out of breath, tactics collapse.
Horikomi: The throw-in. A sacrifice cast inside to destroy the opponent's eye shape.
Oiotoshi: Connect-and-die. Stones in atari that remain lost even when they reach out.
Ishi-no-shita: Under the stones. A tesuji played in the space vacated by captured stones.
Watari: The underpass. Connecting two groups beneath the opponent's stones along the edge.
Nozoki: The peep. A move threatening to cut, usually demanding an immediate connection.
Kikashi: The forcing move. A play that compels a response, gaining advantage in passing.
Shibori: The squeeze. Sacrificial stones that compress the opponent into a collapsed form.
Degiri: Push and cut. A two-move sequence that wedges through, then severs the connection.
Shicho-atari: The ladder breaker. A stone placed in the ladder's path that shatters the pursuit.
Osae: The block. A move that halts the opponent's advance along a line.
Sagari: The descent. A move dropping straight toward the edge to secure a vital liberty.
Katachi: Shape. The form and efficiency of stones — good shape is the soul of strong play.
Korigatachi: Over-concentrated shape. Stones clumped together, wasting their collective power.
Dango: A heavy, eyeless clump. The shape of wasted stones.
Shimari: The corner enclosure. Two stones working together to claim the corner.
Kakari: The approach. A move that challenges a lone enemy corner stone.
Uchikomi: The invasion. A stone played deep inside enemy territory to live or reduce.
Hanami Ko: Flower-viewing ko. A lopsided ko where only one side risks real loss.
Tsuru no Sugomori: The crane's nest. A capturing tesuji that traps stones within their own shape.
Empty Triangle (Aki-sankaku): The mark of inefficiency. Three stones forming a triangle around an empty point.
Cutting Point: The vulnerable seam between stones. Where connections can be severed.
Warikomi: The wedge. A stone inserted between two enemy stones one point apart.
Yurumi Shicho: The loose ladder. A spiraling chase without direct atari that still captures.
Aji Keshi: Destroying your own potential. A move that erases possibilities you could have used later.
Ipponmichi: The one-way street. A forced sequence where every move is the only move.
Hiraki: The extension. Spreading along the side to build a base or claim territory.
Ogeima: The large knight's move. A wider leap than keima — faster but more vulnerable to cutting.
Katatsuki: The shoulder hit. A diagonal approach that presses the opponent down.
Kaketsugi: The hanging connection. An open link that defends the cut while preserving flexibility.
Suji: The line of play. Elegant, correct technique for a tactical sequence.
Honte: The proper move. Solid and sound — resisting the urge to overplay.
Furikawari: The exchange. Trading territory or influence in one area for gains elsewhere.
Chuban: The middle game. The phase of contact fighting and strategic development.
Boshi: The cap. A move placed above the opponent's stone to limit its growth.
Keshi: Erasure. A light move that reduces the opponent's framework without deep invasion.
Kosumi-tsuke: The diagonal attachment. A kick that forces the opponent to choose direction.
Count the liberties before you commit to the fight.
Protect your cutting points before launching an attack.
Fill the outside liberties first in a capturing race.
Strike at the vital point to collapse the opponent's shape.
Seal the corner before you venture to the side.
Approach the corner from the wider side of the board.
Read three moves ahead to verify your path is clear.
Sacrifice the small to secure the large.
Maintain a calm mind to see the entire board.
Practice Tsumego daily to sharpen your inner eye.
Review your games to find the fork in the road.
Strengthen your own groups before invading the opponent’s.
In a ko fight, save the largest threats for the final exchange.
Build through pressure rather than hunting for a direct kill.
Stay light and flexible when playing in enemy territory.
Use thickness as a wall for attack, not as territory.
Verify the ladder path is clear before you begin the chase.
Play the shape move—it often solves multiple problems at once.
Don't play inside a settled position. Look for larger horizons.
Analyze the trades. Do not count corners in isolation.
The enemy's vital point is your vital point.
Capture the cutting stones.
Play away from thickness.
Urgent moves before big moves.
Even a pro connects against a peep.
Don't peep at a cutting point you can cut.
Attack from a distance; do not attach to what you wish to attack.
If the path is unclear, Tenuki.
Lose your first 100 games quickly.
Strange occurrences happen at the 1-2 point.
A rich person should not pick quarrels.
Strike at the head of two stones.
A ponnuki is worth 30 points.
Golden corner, silver side, grass belly.
Learn the Joseki, then forget the Joseki.
Six die, eight live on the second line.
The carpenter's square is alive in the corner.
There is no territory in the center.
The best move is often the simplest one.
Don't chase what you cannot catch.
